editableNewDialogue.text: Can you explain the difference between a binary tree and a binary search tree in simple terms?

Sure. A binary tree is any tree where each node has at most two children (left and right). A binary search tree (BST) is a special kind of binary tree where the values follow an order: all values in the left subtree are smaller than the node, and all values in the right subtree are larger (or follow a consistent rule for equals). That ordering is what makes searching efficient.
Why does that ordering make searching faster?

Because at each node you can discard half the remaining tree: if the target value is smaller, you only need to search the left subtree; if larger, only the right subtree. In a well-balanced BST, this gives about O(log n) search time instead of O(n) in an unordered tree.
Can you give a tiny example of a preorder traversal output?
Example tree: root 10, left child 5, right child 15, and 5 has children 3 and 7. Preorder (root-left-right) visits: 10, 5, 3, 7, 15.
Random question: what was the first message sent on the internet?
A famous early ARPANET message in 1969 was intended to be "LOGIN", but the system crashed after sending only "LO". People often cite "LO" as the first message.
I'm polishing my IELTS Speaking answers, and that "Describe a book" question keeps coming up—could you help me make it sound more natural?
Sure. Paste your draft and I’ll help you make it more fluent, with a clear structure and some natural-sounding details.
Earlier I asked you to replace the book with technical ones just to practice IELTS answers. In real life I don’t read C++ or algorithms books for fun—those were examples only.

Got it—so the C++ manual and "Introduction to Algorithms" choices were just placeholders for practice, not your actual reading preference. I shouldn’t infer that you like technical/programming literature based on those replacements alone.
